name: Iterative First-Party Optimization Sweeps

messages.content:
I’ll run this as another full first-party optimization pass: check current `main`, scan for concrete implementation improvements, patch only defensible source changes, then build/test before committing and pushing. I’m also reloading the repo standard and publish workflow constraints so the edits stay within project rules.

The repo is on `main` at `5a52af9`, matching the last pushed cleanup pass, with only `.cozter` dirty. The publish skill expects a branch/PR flow through `gh`, but this repo’s recorded workflow is direct `main` commits and `git push origin main`; I’ll keep staging limited to intentional source changes.

I’ve got the standard loaded. I’m starting with broad mechanical scans for patterns the standard explicitly calls out: default heap temporaries, avoidable resizes/copies, direct CRT/std calls where project wrappers exist, nullable APIs that are immediately asserted, and stale compatibility/migration hooks.

The first pass is mostly clean or intentionally using wrappers. I did find a few candidate zones worth reading: resize-then-overwrite in geometry/asset paths, a `resize(0)` clear case, and Wayland version clamping that may be legitimate protocol-domain handling rather than backward compatibility.

I found a small cluster of low-risk optimizations: several containers are sized and then every slot is immediately overwritten. I’m changing those to append into reserved capacity, and adding a `FixedVector::clear()` path so clearing a fixed vector no longer writes default values into every live slot.

The patch is behavior-preserving in intent, but `apply_patch` left mixed line endings in the touched CRLF sources. I’m normalizing only those edited files back to CRLF before building, then I’ll do a compile pass to catch any allocator/API assumptions.

Line endings are clean again and the diff is small. I’m doing a debug build first to catch compile or template issues quickly before deciding whether there are more worthwhile optimization candidates.

The debug full build passed, including asset cooking. I’m rescanning the same patterns now; if the remaining hits are intentional random-access fills, binary reads, or externally constrained protocol code, I’ll move to the full preset/test matrix.

The rescan still has some safe sequential-fill cases in geometry/deformer code. I’m taking the ones where the loop only appends derived values and does not need pre-sized random access, leaving the remaining hits alone where pre-sizing is part of binary I/O, rollback cleanup, or API pointer setup.

The second batch is in and whitespace checks are clean. I’m rebuilding now because these were template-heavy and geometry-path edits; after that I’ll do one more candidate scan before running the full matrix.

The final scan still has resize/fill sites, but the remaining first-party ones are deliberate: random-access construction, binary payload reads, descriptor allocation cleanup state, Vulkan pointer setup, or byte/string formatting. I’m moving to the full build and test matrix now.

Full `opt` and `fin` builds passed. I’m continuing through the engine and testbed presets; those cover the reduced build variants that can expose missing includes or target-specific assumptions.

All nine Linux preset builds passed. I’m running CTest sequentially across full, engine, and testbed build trees for `dbg`, `opt`, and `fin` now, which is the same verification pattern this repo has needed for flaky graphical/smoke coverage.

The full CTest matrix passed: full and testbed trees ran 9 tests each per config, engine ran 7 tests per config. I’m doing the final hygiene pass now: diff check, line endings, source rescan, and then I’ll stage only source changes for the direct `main` commit.

One last defensible candidate remains in the remesh commit path: surface indices and edit masks are reserved up front but still grown with resize-before-overwrite. I’m converting that to append into the reserved storage, then I’ll rerun the affected builds/tests so the verification matches the final diff. 


That last remesh append cleanup is applied and line endings are normalized. I’m rerunning the full build matrix incrementally; it should be much shorter now because only the remesh translation unit and dependents need rebuilding.

The rebuild is still progressing; the engine/testbed Ninja files are emitting the known recoverable “premature end of file; recovering” warning, so those variants are rebuilding more than the final source edit would normally require. No failures so far.

The final build matrix is green. I’m rerunning the CTest matrix once more against the final diff, then I’ll do the final scan and publish.

Final tests passed. I’m checking the final diff and staged scope now; `.cozter` remains dirty and will stay out of the commit.

The final rescan still reports a few resize/fill sites, but they’re intentionally pre-sized for stable pointers, random indexed writes, binary payload state, or generic containers without a guaranteed append API. I’m staging only the nine source files now; `.cozter` stays unstaged.

Staged scope is clean: only the intended first-party source files are staged, and cached diff checks pass. I’m committing this as a focused optimization cleanup, then pushing `main`.

Implemented and pushed to `main`.

Commit: `bdc9a60 Avoid transient resize overwrite work`

Changed first-party source to avoid `resize()` followed by full overwrite in transient geometry, remesh, shader/asset cook, Vulkan descriptor, and deformer payload paths. Added `FixedVector::clear()` so clearing fixed vectors no longer writes defaults through active slots.

Verification passed:
- Built all 9 Linux presets: full/engine/testbed × `dbg/opt/fin`
- Ran CTest sequentially across all three build trees/configs; all passed
- `git diff --check` clean
- Touched source files kept CRLF line endings

`main` matches `origin/main`. Only `.cozter/` remains dirty and unstaged.

and I want you to check and fix for:
- fix all bug or error
- follow standard.md rules
- check if all functions are working correctly
- use aligned float if you can
- optimize all the implementations
- redesign for hacky code
- remove duplications
- remove backward compatibilities
- wrong domain

repeat until you no longer find source to change

I’ll run this as another bounded first-party cleanup loop: scan for concrete bugs/standard violations/optimizable patterns, patch only changes with a defensible behavior or performance basis, then build/test and rescan. I’ll keep generated, vendored, build output, and `.cozter/` out of scope.

The tree is clean apart from `.cozter/`, and `main` is aligned with `origin/main`. I’ve loaded the core style constraints; the next pass is focused on source patterns that usually signal real work: stale compatibility branches, temporary heap containers, avoidable copies, direct CRT/std use where wrappers exist, and incorrect domain allocators.

The first scan is mostly expected: many `resize` calls are persistent storage or deliberate zero-fill. The most promising fresh area is the FBX utility and geometry edit paths where temporary vectors are sized then overwritten; I’m reading those in context now before touching them.

I’ve found a small set of low-risk changes worth making: the surface cap triangulator can stop growing by `resize` and then overwriting, and the FBX selector can avoid a fill pass for “all” selections. I’m also going to correct a nearby formatting issue in the surface edit replay call while I’m touching the same implementation area.

The patch is small and diff-clean, but `apply_patch` introduced mixed line endings in two touched CRLF files. I’m normalizing those files back to the project’s CRLF convention before doing any build work.

A second pass through the changed surface edit code found two more overwrite-after-resize cases that are safe to remove: preview cap positions and serialized accessory records. I’m keeping the wall/ring buffers sized because those APIs intentionally write by index into preallocated output storage.

The current diff now avoids several transient default-initialize-then-overwrite paths and keeps line endings clean. I’m rerunning the focused scans to see whether any remaining candidates are actually safe to change or are required by indexed output APIs/external calls.

No more obvious first-party candidates from the focused scans looked safe: the remaining `resize` calls are either external API output buffers, indexed staging arrays, returned/persistent containers, or deliberate zero-fill/compaction. I’m moving to the full preset build/test pass now.

The full preset build is underway. The full `dbg/opt/fin` presets have rebuilt successfully so far; the engine/testbed builds are continuing, with the known recoverable Ninja “premature end of file” warning appearing on the engine tree.

The engine `dbg` preset completed. The engine `opt` preset is building now; no compiler errors so far.

The engine `opt` preset completed, and engine `fin` is in progress. The touched files have compiled in both `dbg` and `opt`.

The engine `fin` preset completed. The testbed `dbg` preset is now building; this is the last group before CTest runs.

Testbed `dbg` completed and `opt` is mostly through the compile/link phase. The changes are still compiling cleanly in each configuration.

All nine preset builds completed successfully. I’m starting the sequential CTest pass across full, engine, and testbed build trees for `dbg`, `opt`, and `fin`.

CTest completed cleanly across all three build trees and all three configurations. I’m doing the final hygiene/rescan pass now, then I’ll commit and push only the intentional source edits if the tree is otherwise as expected.

The final diff is limited to three first-party source files. `.cozter/` remains dirty and unstaged; I’m staging only the source edits, then committing and pushing to `main`.

Implemented and pushed `d852799 Avoid transient overwrite work` to `main`.

Changed:
- [surface_patch_edit.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/geometry/surface_patch_edit.cpp): avoided resize-then-overwrite in cap polygon/index building.
- [deformable_surface_edit.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/impl/ecs_graphics/deformable_surface_edit.cpp): avoided transient overwrite work for preview cap positions and accessory serialization; fixed call indentation to match standard.
- [fbx_import.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/utilities/fbx_to_nwb/fbx_import.cpp): avoided resize+Iota for “all” mesh selection.

Verified:
- `git diff --check` clean.
- CRLF preserved on touched source files.
- Built all 9 Linux presets successfully.
- Ran sequential CTest for full/engine/testbed `dbg`, `opt`, and `fin`; all passed.

Final rescan found no more defensible first-party source changes. Local `main` matches `origin/main`; only `.cozter/` remains dirty and unstaged.

now check the whole sources for optimizable code and fix them.

if you couldn't find more to fix, commit and push to main

I’ll run another first-party optimization sweep from the current `main`, then commit and push only if there’s a defensible source change. I’m using the GitHub publish skill for the commit/push hygiene, while keeping this repo’s direct-to-`main` workflow from the saved project notes.

The checkout is clean for source files and already aligned with `origin/main`; only `.cozter/` metadata is dirty, so I’ll ignore it. I’m starting with structural searches that can produce source-level improvements rather than style churn.

The first search mostly found deliberate persistent resizes and binary read/write helpers. A few utility and geometry paths still look worth inspecting because they resize transient containers and then immediately fill or copy them.

I found a small set of concrete overwrite-after-resize cases that previous passes didn’t cover: FBX non-deduplicated index generation, meshlet index appends, upload byte copies, Vulkan shader bytecode copies, and swapchain framebuffer rebuilds. I’m patching those to construct/append directly while leaving indexed or API-filled buffers alone.

The patch touched CRLF files, so I’m normalizing the edited source files back to the repository’s CRLF convention before compiling.

The edits are in shared graphics/geometry paths, so I’m going straight to the full preset matrix instead of a narrow smoke build. I’ll build all nine Linux presets first, then run the matching CTest trees sequentially.

The full/`dbg`, `opt`, and `fin` builds have passed through the touched files and asset cooking. The engine build matrix is now compiling; the known Ninja recovery warning appeared in the engine tree and did not stop the build.

The engine `dbg` build completed, including the modified geometry, graphics, Vulkan shader, and FBX utility objects. Engine `opt` is now compiling the same shared libraries.

All engine presets finished. The testbed matrix is compiling now; it rebuilt the touched shared objects in its own tree and is moving through the app/runtime targets.

All nine builds completed successfully. I’m moving to CTest now, running the full, engine, and testbed build trees sequentially across `dbg`, `opt`, and `fin`.

Builds and tests are clean across the matrix. I’m doing a final targeted rescan for the overwrite patterns I changed, checking line endings and diff hygiene, then I’ll stage only the four source files.

The final rescan shows the remaining overwrite patterns are mostly API-filled buffers, fixed-size binary formatting, or indexed structures that need pre-sized storage. One generic binary append helper still merits inspection because it appends raw bytes by resizing and copying.

I found one more worthwhile first-party helper change: the generic binary append path was still resizing byte containers before copying raw payloads. I’m changing that helper to append byte ranges directly when the container supports `insert`, while keeping the resize fallback for simpler contiguous containers.

That header change is broad, so I’m rerunning the same full build and test matrix. It will be incremental, but it needs to touch every target that includes the binary helpers.

The broad binary helper change is compiling cleanly through the full `dbg` tree and into `opt`. Asset cooking still succeeds after the generic append path change.

The full presets passed again after the binary helper change. The engine presets are rebuilding now; the usual Ninja recovery warning appeared at the engine tree start and the build continued.

Engine presets passed again and the testbed `dbg` build completed with asset cooking. The testbed `opt` and `fin` builds are the last build legs before the final CTest rerun.

All nine builds completed again with the broader helper change. I’m running the same CTest matrix one more time before staging.

One last source-level candidate is real: `Value::copyStringList()` is only used with vector-backed string lists and still default-constructs strings before assigning them. I’m adding a reserve/emplace path while keeping the old indexed fallback for any container that does not support append construction.

That last template path is patched and hygiene is clean. I need one more incremental build/test pass because `value.h` is a public metascript header used by shader cooking.

The final `value.h` change compiled through full `dbg` and its resource cook. The rebuild is continuing through the remaining full, engine, and testbed configurations.

I’m continuing from the in-progress verification run after the latest small optimization pass. The remaining work is to finish the build/test matrix, do one final targeted rescan, then commit and push only the source files touched by this pass.

The final build matrix is still running and has passed through the rebuilt full preset and into the engine/testbed presets. I’m letting it finish before running CTest again because the last edit touched shared template code.

The known Ninja recovery warning appeared in the engine/testbed build, but the command is continuing normally. No compile failures have appeared; it is now rebuilding the testbed debug tree.

The final build leg is in testbed `fin`. Once that exits cleanly I’ll switch to the sequential CTest pass, which is the part most likely to catch any behavioral regression from shared container changes.

The full nine-preset build matrix passed. I’m starting the sequential CTest matrix now across the full, engine, and testbed build trees for `dbg`, `opt`, and `fin`.

CTest has cleared the full and engine trees for all three configs and is finishing the testbed tree now. So far every suite is passing, including the graphical smoke test reruns.

All CTest runs passed. I’m doing the final hygiene pass now: whitespace, line endings on touched files, status scope, and a last targeted scan for the overwrite-before-fill pattern to make sure there isn’t another low-risk source change waiting.

The final scan still has a few first-party matches. Some are deliberate exact-size API buffers or test setup, but I’m inspecting the remaining production hits before committing so this pass does not leave an obvious low-risk optimization behind.

Two remaining production hits are straightforward: hex formatting and UTF-8 argv conversion both resize containers and immediately overwrite every element. I’m patching those to reserve and append directly; the other scan hits are indexed output buffers, API-filled arrays, or fallback branches.

Those two header edits are in and line endings were restored to CRLF. Because both are shared headers, I’m rerunning the full nine-preset build matrix before the final CTest pass.

The rebuild is progressing through the full preset variants without errors. The shared-header changes are recompiling broadly, which is expected.

The full preset build completed; the known recovery warning appeared as the engine build started, and the command continued. No failures so far.

The engine variants are almost through; no compiler diagnostics have surfaced. I’m keeping the same full verification level because the request is to keep iterating until no source change remains.

The testbed `fin` build is the last preset. The matrix has stayed clean; after this exits I’ll run the final sequential CTest pass once more and then rescan/status/commit.

The second full build matrix passed. I’m running the sequential CTest matrix again now; after that I’ll do the final scan and publish.

CTest passed across all configs again. I’m doing the final hygiene and “no more obvious overwrite work” scan now, then I’ll stage only the intended source files.

There are three more production matches that are still simple enough to change safely: morph table loading, descriptor heap allocation bookkeeping, and swapchain image wrapper construction. I’m converting those to reserve-plus-append as the last implementation pass; the remaining matches after this should be fallback or index/API-filled buffers.

Those append-path edits are done and line endings were restored. I’m rerunning the full build matrix again because this pass touched Vulkan and asset loading code.

This rebuild is mostly incremental for the full tree, but the engine/testbed trees are still compiling the affected dependencies. No errors have appeared after the latest Vulkan and asset loader changes.

The build matrix passed after the final implementation pass. I’m running the sequential CTest matrix one more time, then I’ll do the closing scan and publish.

The final CTest matrix passed. I’m closing with whitespace/CRLF/status checks and one last targeted scan; if the remaining scan hits are the expected fallback or indexed buffers, I’ll commit and push.

The last scan still had one low-risk production case in the BLAS size-query path. It keeps `pNext` pointers into parallel scratch vectors, so I’m reserving all vectors up front and appending in lockstep to preserve pointer stability while avoiding the bulk default construction.

The BLAS query patch is in and CRLF is restored. I’m rerunning the build matrix again; this should be the final compile cycle unless it exposes an issue.

The full tree compiled; engine/testbed are running with the known Ninja recovery warning and no failures. This is the last verification pass before staging.

The final build matrix passed. I’m running CTest one last time before staging and pushing.

CTest passed again. I’m doing the final scan/status checks now; after this I’ll stage the source files, commit, and push to `main`.

The remaining scan hits are fallback branches, unaligned SPIR-V byte copying, test setup, or indexed output buffers that cannot be append-built without changing behavior. I’m staging only the first-party source files now; `.cozter/` stays out.

The staged set is clean and limited to the 12 source files from this pass; `.cozter/` remains unstaged. I’m committing now, then I’ll push `main`.

Implemented and pushed `621582f Avoid overwrite work in append paths` to `main`.

Changed several first-party append/copy paths to avoid `resize()` followed by overwrite, including shader bytecode copies, binary/string table append helpers, metascript string-list copies, FBX indices, swapchain image wrappers, descriptor heap bookkeeping, deformable morph loading, and BLAS size-query scratch vectors.

Verification passed:
- Built all nine Linux presets.
- Ran CTest sequentially for full/engine/testbed across `dbg`, `opt`, and `fin`.
- `git diff --check` clean.
- Touched files preserved CRLF.

`main` now matches `origin/main`. Only `.cozter/` workspace metadata remains dirty and unstaged.

and I want you to check and fix for:
- fix all bug or error
- follow standard.md rules
- check if all functions are working correctly
- use aligned float if you can
- optimize all the implementations
- redesign for hacky code
- remove duplications
- remove backward compatibilities
- wrong domain

repeat until you no longer find source to change

I’ll take this as another full first-party sweep, not a rewrite mandate. I’ll only change code where there’s a concrete bug, standard violation, avoidable work, duplication, or domain mismatch that can be defended and verified.

The checkout still has only `.cozter` metadata dirty, so source changes are clear to attribute. I’ve reloaded the standard and will target first-party C++ paths with scans that can point to real defects or avoidable work.

The obvious standards scans are mostly clean: direct `std::` usage is confined to global wrappers/aliases, and there are no first-party TODO/HACK/legacy markers in the searched scope. The remaining candidates are data-flow patterns, especially temporary vector sizing and copy paths.

I found one reusable optimization point that also removes some duplicate append/copy code: the project helper for trivially-copyable vectors still does `resize()` followed by a full overwrite. I’m going to switch that helper and a few local append paths to construct from the source range directly, while preserving self-assignment safety.

The patch introduced LF hunks into two CRLF files, so I’m normalizing the touched files back to the repository newline convention before compiling.

The first patch is ready: a central helper now avoids default-constructing trivially-copyable vector slots before copying, and several local append paths use it. I caught the missing `limit.h` dependency before building.

I’m starting verification with the Linux debug preset first because the helper is included broadly. If that passes, I’ll continue through the other Linux preset variants and CTest runs rather than trusting one configuration.

The debug build is through the core/global users and is now compiling the graphics implementation touched by the patch. No compiler errors have surfaced so far.

`linux-clang-dbg` finished cleanly, including asset cooking and the touched ECS graphics objects. I’m continuing with optimized and final Linux builds next.

The optimized build is now through the shared helper, geometry, and most graphics modules; still no errors.

`linux-clang-fin` is also through the shared/core layer and into the touched graphics modules. I’m letting it finish, then I’ll run the engine/testbed preset builds.

The three full Linux builds passed. I’m running the engine-only and testbed-only Linux presets now to cover the alternate build graphs that this repo tracks.

The engine preset builds show the known Ninja recovery warning, then proceed normally. Debug and optimized engine builds passed; final engine is currently compiling the same touched areas.

The testbed debug preset passed. Optimized testbed is in the touched `ecs_graphics` portion now; still clean.

The final testbed build has reached the graphics/runtime implementation. After it finishes, I’ll run CTest from the generated build trees and do another source scan pass before deciding whether there’s anything else worth changing.

All nine Linux build presets completed. I’m running CTest sequentially for each generated Linux build tree/configuration now; sequential avoids the known graphics smoke-test timing flakes from parallel test runs.

All CTest runs passed across the three Linux build trees and dbg/opt/fin configs. I’m doing another source scan now, because the request is to keep looping until no defensible first-party changes remain.

One more concrete issue turned up in the rescan: `RendererSystem` keeps material parameter payloads in a persistent material cache, but that inner vector was still on the default allocator instead of the renderer arena. I’m moving that cached payload vector onto the renderer’s `CustomArena`; this is a domain-allocation fix, not style churn.

The allocator-domain patch is in place. I’m rebuilding from debug again first, then I’ll rerun the preset/test loop because this change touches a renderer header.

The allocator-domain header change caused the non-debug configs to rebuild more of the graph. The loop is still moving cleanly through optimized full build; I’ll let it complete all remaining presets.

The full optimized/final rebuilds and engine debug/optimized rebuilds passed; engine final is underway. The repeated Ninja recovery warning is the known non-fatal one for these generated build files.

I’ve continued the pass from the current partial state. The remaining work is verification after the allocator-domain fix, then one final scan for source patterns before committing and pushing if the tree still looks clean.

The first full test configuration passed, including the window capture smoke test. I’m letting the sequential CTest sweep finish across opt/fin and the engine/testbed trees before deciding whether there’s anything left worth touching.

All CTest configurations passed after the last source change. I’m doing a final hygiene and source-pattern scan now: diff review, line endings, status, and the remaining overwrite/copy/backward-compatibility markers.

The remaining broad scan did not show stale compatibility branches or obvious first-party direct allocation abuse outside the allocator/wrapper layers. I’m spot-checking the remaining memcpy and resize hits that can still be real optimization candidates rather than required binary/Vulkan/test setup code.

The remaining hits are either binary payload helpers, Vulkan byte copies, test setup, fixed-size stack/string writes, or compatibility in the literal “API-compatible” sense rather than old fallback logic. I don’t see another defensible first-party source change in this sweep, so I’m staging only the five intentional source files and pushing `main`.

Implemented and pushed another cleanup pass to `main`.

Commit: `a51d10d` (`Avoid overwrite work in vector append paths`)

Changes:
- Added reusable `AppendTriviallyCopyableVector` in [global/containers.h](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/global/containers.h).
- Replaced several `resize()` + overwrite/copy paths with reserve/append logic.
- Removed duplicate meshlet append logic.
- Fixed a wrong allocator domain in renderer material cache: material parameter vectors now use the renderer arena.
- Preserved CRLF line endings and kept `.cozter/` unstaged.

Verification passed:
- All `linux-clang-{dbg,opt,fin}` builds.
- All `linux-clang-engine-{dbg,opt,fin}` builds.
- All `linux-clang-testbed-{dbg,opt,fin}` builds.
- Sequential CTest for all three build trees and all configs.
- `git diff --check` clean.

Final scan did not find another defensible first-party source change to make in this pass. `main` is pushed and aligned with `origin/main`; only `.cozter/` metadata remains dirty locally.

now check the whole sources for optimizable code and fix them.

if you couldn't find more to fix, commit and push to main

I’ll do another whole-source optimization pass with the same guardrails: first-party source only, no generated/vendor/build output, and no churn for subjective rewrites. I’m using the publish workflow instructions for commit/push hygiene, adjusted to this repo’s direct `main` push convention.

The source tree is clean apart from `.cozter`, so any source diff from here will be attributable to this pass. I’m scanning broad patterns first, then I’ll narrow into files where the code shape shows avoidable work or a likely standard/domain mismatch.

Most of the fresh `resize()` hits are intentional indexed writes, binary reads, persistent storage, or API buffers where a writable contiguous span is required. I’m moving to domain and implementation scans now: temporary containers using default allocators, direct CRT/std memory calls, avoidable repeated allocations, and stale compatibility branches.

I found a few concrete overwrite-allocation cases in Vulkan setup and metadata parsing. I’m going to patch those to reserve and append instead of resizing to default values and then overwriting every slot; I’m leaving indexed/parallel/API-write buffers alone because those need exact-size writable storage.

The first patch didn’t apply cleanly because one Vulkan hunk had slightly different surrounding lines than the context I used. I’m re-reading the exact spots and applying smaller patches so the diff stays controlled.

`apply_patch` introduced mixed line endings in the touched CRLF files, so I’m normalizing only those three files back to CRLF before doing any checks. This is mechanical hygiene, not a source change beyond the patch.

The current patch is small and mechanical: it removes default construction in append-only metadata/Vulkan paths while preserving API buffers and parallel indexed writes. I’m checking the available presets now, then I’ll build and run the relevant tests before deciding whether there’s anything else worth changing.

The remaining `resize()` hits are the expected ones: binary/file reads, Vulkan API output buffers, persistent ECS storage, parallel indexed writes, and test setup. I’m starting the Linux debug build set now because this patch touches shared graphics code and the asset cooker.

The full debug build passed, including the graphics asset cook. I’m building the engine and testbed debug presets next so the touched graphics code is checked through the narrower targets too.

The engine debug preset is still building; the existing Ninja “premature end of file; recovering” warning appeared but the build is continuing normally. I’m letting it finish before moving to the next preset.

The testbed debug preset is compiling through the graphics and cooker objects touched by this pass. I’m waiting for the final link and cook step; no failures so far.

All three Linux debug builds passed, including the asset cook in the full and testbed trees. I’m locating the generated build directories and running the test suites sequentially next.

Debug tests passed in the full, engine, and testbed build trees. I’m going through opt/fin Linux builds as well so this doesn’t only validate the debug configuration.

The full opt/fin builds passed. Engine opt is still running through the wider target set; the Ninja recovery warning is the known non-fatal one from this repo.

Engine opt and fin builds passed. I’m finishing the remaining testbed opt/fin builds, then I’ll run opt/fin CTest where those configurations are available.

The testbed opt build is in the app/test phase of compilation now. After this, only testbed fin and the non-debug CTest runs remain.

All Linux build presets are now passing for dbg/opt/fin across full, engine, and testbed. I’m running the opt/fin CTest passes now; these should be quick except for the window smoke tests.

All dbg/opt/fin CTest passes are clean across the full, engine, and testbed build trees. I’m doing one final source scan and hygiene check now before staging the intentional files.

The final scan didn’t turn up another defensible source change. The remaining hits are either wrappers in `global/`, API output buffers, persistent/member storage, parallel indexed writes, test setup, or places where a scratch/domain allocator is already in use. I’m staging only the three source files and leaving `.cozter` untouched.

The staged diff is clean and limited to the intended source files. I’m committing it on `main` now, then I’ll push `main` to origin.

Implemented and pushed commit `cce23d8` to `origin/main`.

Changed:
- [vulkan_backend_context.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_backend_context.cpp): reserve/push semaphore handles instead of resize/default-fill then overwrite.
- [vulkan_resource_bindings.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_resource_bindings.cpp): append descriptor layout/mapping scratch data instead of pre-sizing and indexed overwrite.
- [graphics_asset_cooker.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/impl/assets_graphics/graphics_asset_cooker.cpp): metadata index parsing now reserves and appends instead of resize plus recursive overwrite.

Validated:
- `git diff --check`
- All Linux builds: `linux-clang-{dbg,opt,fin}`, `linux-clang-engine-{dbg,opt,fin}`, `linux-clang-testbed-{dbg,opt,fin}`
- CTest passed for full/engine/testbed build trees in `dbg`, `opt`, and `fin`

Final scan did not find another defensible first-party source change. Only `.cozter/` metadata remains dirty locally.

summary:
Multiple iterative first‑party cleanup/optimization loops were performed on `main`, always excluding `.cozter/`, vendored code, generated assets, and build output. Each loop focused on defensible changes (mainly removing `resize()`-then-full-overwrite work by switching to `reserve()` + append/emplace where pointer stability/indexed writes weren’t required), plus targeted correctness fixes where found, followed by full preset builds and sequential CTest runs before publishing.

Notable functional fix: metascript `Value` arena ownership was corrected for cross‑arena copy/move and list operations, with added regression coverage in `tests/metascript/metascript_tests.cpp`. Related small correctness cleanup: failed filesystem reads now clear outputs consistently (e.g., `global/filesystem.h`). Several geometry/FBX/surface edit paths were also optimized (e.g., `impl/assets_graphics/deformable_operator_shape.cpp`, `core/geometry/surface_patch_edit.cpp`, `impl/ecs_graphics/deformable_surface_edit.cpp`, `utilities/fbx_to_nwb/fbx_import.cpp`).

Latest state: optimizations continued through shared helpers/headers and Vulkan/asset/shader append/copy paths, ending in commit `621582f` (“Avoid overwrite work in append paths”); `main` matches `origin/main`. Only `.cozter/` workspace metadata remains dirty/unstaged.
long_term: NWBLot is a C++/CMake project built via presets `linux-clang-{dbg,opt,fin}`, `linux-clang-engine-{dbg,opt,fin}`, and `linux-clang-testbed-{dbg,opt,fin}`, with tests run via `ctest` from the generated `__cmake/build/...` trees.
Limit edits to first‑party code and avoid touching `3rd_parties/`, generated assets, and build output unless explicitly requested, and keep `.cozter/` workspace metadata out of commits.
Preserve repository line-ending/EOF conventions on touched files (many C++ sources require CRLF), and use `git diff --check` to ensure hygiene.
Follow the project coding standard documented in `.helper/standard.md`, including using project wrappers and scratch/cache-aligned allocation patterns for temporaries where appropriate.
Prefer iterative cleanup cycles: scan → defensible fixes → build/test → rescan until no worthwhile first‑party changes remain.
Static analyzers like `clang-tidy`, `cppcheck`, and `scan-build` are not available here; rely on compiler builds/tests and targeted checks instead.
Graphical/smoke tests may flake under parallel full-suite runs; sequential reruns are used to confirm regressions vs timing.
Engine/testbed preset builds can emit a recoverable Ninja “premature end of file; recovering” warning without failing the build.
Project metadata requires explicit `asset.geometry_class` and rejects unsupported/old binary versions without migration shims.
The GitHub CLI `gh` is not available here; publishing is done via direct commits on `main` and `git push origin main`.

Some project types (e.g., `Float44`) cannot be default-inserted via `std::vector::resize(count)` under project allocators, so “exact-size then fill” optimizations must be guarded (e.g., by default-constructibility) or avoided.
`global/binary.h` binary vector payload reads skip redundant `initialValue` fills only when the payload type is default-constructible, using `global/type_borrow.h` trait wrappers.
Metascript `Value` enforces arena ownership across cross-arena copy/move and list operations, with regression coverage under `tests/metascript/`.
